result:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how strange it is to be conscious in 2025. Not in the usual “the world is burning and we’re all doomed” way—though that current is always there—but in a quieter, almost absurd sense: we are the first generation that has to consciously decide how much of our attention we’re willing to rent out to machines that want to eat our time.

Every morning I wake up, pick up a rectangle of glass and metal, and within thirty seconds I’ve been offered dopamine hits from twelve different sources. A friend’s vacation photo. A rage-inducing headline. A video of a raccoon stealing a bottle of hot sauce. A notification that someone liked a post I wrote three years ago. The sheer volume of it feels like standing under a waterfall made of other people’s thoughts. And the weirdest part is that I paid $1,200 for the privilege.

I keep wondering what a medieval monk would make of us. Not the romanticized version who spent all day illuminated manuscripts and smelling of incense, but the real ones—people who chose to live inside strict rules of silence, repetition, and attention because they believed the undisciplined mind was a danger to the soul. They built monasteries with bells that rang at precise intervals to yank their attention back to God. We built smartphones with bells that yank our attention back to capitalism, outrage, and the curated lives of people we don’t actually know.

The difference is they chose their bells.

I’ve started doing this ridiculous thing where I leave my phone in another room for whole stretches of the day. Not as some noble digital-minimalist performance, but because I noticed I was becoming a person I didn’t particularly like. The version of me that checks the news first thing in the morning is jittery and cynical before his feet even touch the floor. The version that doomscrolls at 1 a.m. is a paranoid, jealous little gremlin who thinks everyone else has figured out how to live except him. I want to be the version who stares at the ceiling thinking about how moss grows on trees or why clouds look like they’re moving so slowly when they’re actually racing at highway speeds.

There’s a strange liberation in being slightly bored again. Boredom used to be my enemy. Now it feels like a friend who shows up unannounced and says, “Hey, remember how to think your own thoughts?” I’ve started carrying a small notebook again—not for profound insights, but for the stupid ones. Yesterday I wrote: “The way seagulls scream at each other sounds exactly like drunk people arguing about whose turn it is to buy the next round.” That observation is useless. It will never make me money. It probably won’t even make a good tweet. But it felt like mine.

I’ve also been thinking about how nostalgia works now. When I was a kid, nostalgia was something that happened to old people. Now I get waves of it about 2009. 2009! That wasn’t even that long ago, but it feels like another civilization. A time before the timeline, before the constant quantification of life. People still listened to whole albums. You could be unreachable for hours and no one assumed you were dead. Your personality wasn’t expected to be a personal brand. If you took a photo, you had to wait to get it developed, and when you did, half the pictures were of someone’s thumb.

I don’t want to go back. I just want to notice what we traded away without realizing the price.

The other night I was walking through my neighborhood at dusk and saw a little girl teaching her younger brother how to ride a bike. She was so patient with him, running alongside, holding the seat, shouting encouragement every time he wobbled. It was completely ordinary. It was also one of the most beautiful things I’d seen in months. I almost cried, which is insane. But I think I was crying for the version of myself that used to notice things like that without having to remind myself to notice. The version that wasn’t constantly performing observation for some future post.

We talk a lot about attention as if it’s a resource we need to “protect.” That framing is too clean. Attention is closer to identity. What you pay attention to is, quite literally, who you become. If you pay attention to cruelty, you become crueler. If you pay attention to performance, you become performative. If you pay attention to the way light falls through leaves at 4:47pm in early October, you become… someone who notices light. Someone slower. Someone a little more useless in the eyes of the market, perhaps. But maybe more human.

I’ve been trying to become that second kind of person. It’s harder than it should be. The current is so strong in the other direction. Every piece of technology I own was designed by people who went to Stanford and read “Hooked” by Nir Eyal and genuinely believe they’re making the world better by making it more addictive. They call it “frictionless.” I call it soul erosion with better marketing.

But here’s the hopeful part: the fact that I can even see it means the machines haven’t won completely. The fact that I can choose, even for a few hours a day, to be unreachable, to be bored, to be present with the ridiculous miracle of being alive—that’s still possible. It’s just not the default anymore. The default is optimized. We have to fight for the unoptimized life now.

So I’m fighting in small, almost pathetic ways. I read physical books with no internet connection. I write longhand sometimes just to remember what it feels like for thoughts to move as slow as my hand. I talk to strangers in coffee shops even when it feels awkward. I listen to music without doing anything else at the same time. I watch the way my cat decides which patch of sunlight to occupy like it’s the most important decision in the universe, and I try to learn from her.

None of this will change the world. The algorithms will keep getting better. The attention merchants will keep getting richer. The water will keep rising and the politics will keep getting uglier and the raccoons will keep stealing hot sauce.

But I refuse to become a node in someone else’s attention economy without my consent.

I want to be difficult to sell to. I want to be unprofitable. I want to have thoughts that no one ever reads, memories that were never photographed, jokes that were only funny to the two people in the room. I want to waste time gloriously. I want to be the kind of person a medieval monk might recognize as a fellow creature—flawed, distracted, but at least trying to ring his own bells sometimes.

The sun is coming up as I write this. There’s a bird outside making a sound like it’s clearing its throat. My coffee is getting cold. My phone is in the other room, probably vibrating with things that want my attention.

It can want.

I’m busy noticing the light.

(Word count: 1,012)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
